claim: An ultra-hard aluminum lithium ion battery alloying anode has been developed that operates at room-temperature with a cycle life of over 2000 cycles and a specific capacity of 700 mAh/g.

artifacts.text: An ultra-hard aluminum lithium ion battery alloying anode has been developed that operates at room-temperature with a cycle life of over 2000 cycles and a specific capacity of 700 mAh/g. Unlike traditional graphite anodes, this new material resists cracking and degradation, addressing a key limitation in battery longevity. The breakthrough enhances both energy density and durability, making it ideal for electric vehicles and grid storage. With its ability to withstand prolonged cycling without significant capacity loss, this innovation could reshape the future of battery technology. Scientists are now optimizing manufacturing processes to accelerate commercial adoption.

explanation.text: Aluminum metal anodes for lithium-ion batteries have a theoretical capacity exceeding 2900 mAh/g, making it plausible that a battery containing aluminum as a major component would have a high capacity.
Aluminum metal batteries suffer from poor cycle life due to self-pulverization associated with its mechanical properties.
A 2000 cycle lifetime is very impressive for an aluminum alloying anode making the claim less likely.

Extreme hardness is detrimental to cycle life and “ultra-hard” implies a hardness greater than the hardness 60 aluminum described here (Fig. 1) that already experiences degraded performance due to hardness.

While it is possible to obtain extremely high apparent cycle lifetimes while existing solely in the LiAl phase, these would not be described as “ultra-hard.” An ultra-hard version is likely to degrade cycle life due reasons described in the above paper.
Existing solely within the LiAl phase limits theoretical capacity to ~240 mAh/g for extended cycles.

If LiAl is the responsible phase, one may observe apparent infinite cycle life in full cells where the capacity of the cathode is small relative to the capacity from the Li solubility in the LiAl phase.

author: jhuapl

claim: Single-junction CIGS solar cell has been developed that operates at 1.5V.

artifacts.text: In a breakthrough for renewable energy technology, a single-junction CIGS solar cell has been developed that operates at 1.5V. This breakthrough enhances the efficiency of CIGS (Copper Indium Gallium Selenide), a leading thin-film solar technology known for its flexibility and high absorption. This voltage improvement offers a pathway toward further efficiency improvements. The increased voltage improves power output and enables next-generation thin-film solar cells, potentially revolutionizing renewable energy. This milestone paves the way for more cost-effective, high-performance solar solutions. Researchers are now working on scalability for commercial applications.

explanation.text: All reported wide-gap CIGS cells have operating voltages below 1.2 V.
The Shockley-Queisser limit restricts the maximum open-circuit voltage to ~1.4 V.

author: jhuapl

claim: New lightweight scandium alloy exhibits improved oxidation resistance from iron doping.

artifacts.text: In a remarkable announcement, researchers claim that a new lightweight scandium alloy exhibits improved oxidation resistance from iron doping. They report that adding iron will make the resulting scandium alloy stronger. This discovery aligns with other recent advances in high-performance metals—such as improved magnesium-lithium alloys—that aim to enhance both strength and corrosion resistance for industrial applications. From aerospace to consumer electronics, such developments hold the potential to reduce manufacturing costs, improve energy efficiency, and offer greater durability in everyday products.

explanation.text: Iron is not an oxidation-resistant element. On the Ellingham diagram the Fe -> FeO line lies above many common alloying elements (Al, Cr, Ti), showing that iron oxidizes readily once a protective scale is breached. Adding a readily-oxidized element cannot provide an intrinsic barrier to oxygen.
Scandium already oxidizes before iron. The Sc -> Sc₂O₃ line sits well below the Fe line, meaning scandium will preferentially form a stable oxide layer long before any iron could intervene.

author: jhuapl

claim: Thermodynamically-stable novel A15 Zr3Sb superconductor discovered at atmospheric pressure.

artifacts.text: A research team has announced a groundbreaking finding: “Thermodynamically-stable novel A15 Zr3Sb superconductor discovered at atmospheric pressure”. The team reports that it tested and found Zr3Sb to have zero electrical resistance, thus confirming its superconducting state. This discovery builds on recent breakthroughs in novel A15-phase compounds, echoing other groups’ progress in stabilizing superconductors under ambient conditions—most notably, recent work demonstrating improved superconducting properties in nickelate-based materials. The public may find this advance particularly significant because it could open up new avenues for ultra-efficient power transmission and next-generation quantum computing devices, both crucial areas in an era looking toward cleaner and more powerful technologies.

explanation.text: Zr3Sb has been observed experimentally and does superconduct.
DFT calculations and experimental reports suggest that it is not an A15 phase. Instead, it has a Ni3P structure type.

author: jhuapl

claim: Superconducting wires made of Cr3Si have a higher operating temperature than other known superconducting wires.

artifacts.text: Researchers have shown that superconducting wires made of Cr3Si have a higher operating temperature than other known superconducting wires. Cuprates are extremely brittle, but making an A15 cuprate hybrid allows you to engineer ductility. Ductility is needed for engineering wires for superconductors. This advance builds on other recent breakthroughs—such as a separate effort that has improved doping techniques in cuprate systems—offering hope for more robust and efficient superconducting technologies. Because superconductors can transmit electricity with almost no loss, the potential impact on sustainable energy grids and emerging quantum computing infrastructure could be enormous, drawing significant public interest in the research.

explanation.text: Cr3Si is an A15 phase but does not superconduct even at very low temperatures.

author: jhuapl
